title: Psalms 67
subtitle: May God Be Gracious to Us and Bless Us

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “May Your Ways Be Known on Earth”

heb.paragraph: "May God be gracious to us and bless us (yəbārek̅ēnū) and make his face shine on us — so that your ways may be known on earth, your salvation among all nations" (vv.1–2). The Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24–26) turned outward: Israel is blessed SO THAT the nations may know God. Blessing is not terminal but instrumental. The refrain: "May the peoples praise you, God; may all the peoples praise you" (vv.3, 5).

notes.note: "May God bless us... so that your ways may be known on earth." The "so that" makes Israel’s blessing missionary. Blessed to be a blessing (Gen 12:2–3).
"May the peoples praise you, God. May the nations be glad and sing for joy, for you rule the peoples with equity." Universal praise, universal joy, universal justice. The psalm’s vision is global.

refs.note: The psalm opens by adapting the Aaronic blessing, extending priestly benediction to include mission.
Being a light to the Gentiles fulfills the psalm's vision of God's ways known among all nations.
The gospel announced beforehand to Abraham that all nations would be blessed echoes this psalm.

hist.context: Psalm 67 adapts the priestly blessing (Num 6:24-26) for congregational use, adding missional purpose: Israel is blessed 'so that your ways may be known on earth.' The refrain 'may the peoples praise you' (vv.3, 5) envisions universal worship. This short psalm has been called 'the Old Testament Lord's Prayer' for its combination of blessing and mission.

sections.header: Verses 5–7 — “The Earth Has Yielded Its Harvest”

heb.paragraph: "The land yields its harvest (yəbūl); God, our God, blesses us" (v.6). The harvest as evidence of blessing. But the point is not the harvest itself: "May God bless us still, so that all the ends of the earth will fear him" (v.7). The final word is global: all the ends of the earth. The harvest is a sign; the mission is the point.

notes.note: "May the peoples praise you. The land yields its harvest; God blesses us." Harvest → blessing → praise. The agricultural cycle is theological.
"May God bless us still, so that all the ends of the earth will fear him." The psalm’s final vision: blessing Israel until the entire world recognises God. The most missionary psalm in Book II.

refs.note: The great multitude from every nation fulfills the psalm's vision of all peoples praising God.
All nations blessed through the king develops the same Abrahamic promise this psalm assumes.
Nations streaming to Zion to learn God's ways parallels the psalm's missionary vision.

hist.context: The harvest blessing (v.6: 'the land yields its harvest') suggests use at agricultural festivals, but the purpose transcends material blessing — 'that all the ends of the earth will fear him.' The psalm reinterprets Israel's election: chosenness serves universal mission. Some scholars consider it an eschatological hymn anticipating the ingathering of nations.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: Gracious provider whose blessing flows through Israel to all nations (vv.1-2,6-7)
ppl.text: The opening echoes the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24-26): 'May God be gracious to us and bless us and make his face shine on us' (v.1). But the purpose clause transforms it from national to missional: 'so that your ways may be known on earth, your salvation among all nations' (v.2). Israel's blessing is instrumental — designed to reveal God to the world. The earth 'yields its harvest' (v.6) as God blesses, connecting agricultural prosperity to international witness. This theology of blessing-as-mission anticipates the Abrahamic covenant's 'all nations blessed through you' (Gen 12:3).

ppl.name: The Nations (All Peoples)
ppl.role: Universal audience invited to praise and governed justly (vv.3-5)
ppl.text: The refrain 'May the peoples praise you, God; may all the peoples praise you' (vv.3,5) appears twice, framing the central claim: 'You rule the peoples with equity and guide the nations of the earth' (v.4). This is not forced subjugation but just governance — God's rule produces gladness and singing among the nations (v.4). The psalm envisions a world where every people group finds joy in God's reign, making it one of the most universalist texts in the Hebrew Bible.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMay God bless us still, so that all the ends of the earth will fear him.ESVGod shall bless us; let all the ends of the earth fear him!NIVThe land yields its harvest; God, our God, blesses us.ESVThe earth has yielded its increase; God, our God, shall bless us.

rec.text: ""May God bless us... so that your ways may be known on earth." The "so that" makes Israel’s blessing missionary. Blessed to be a blessing (Gen 12:2–3)."

rows.label: The Harvest Vision — 67:5–7
rows.text: "The land yields its harvest." "May all the ENDS OF THE EARTH fear him."

chiasm.title: Psalm 67 — Concentric Praise of the Nations

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: May God be gracious to us and bless us and make his face shine on us (v.1)
pairs.bottom: May God bless us still, so that all the ends of the earth will fear him (v.7)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: So that your ways may be known on earth, your salvation among all nations (v.2)
pairs.bottom: The land yields its harvest; God, our God, blesses us (v.6)

pairs.label: C
pairs.top: May the peoples praise you, God; may all the peoples praise you (v.3)
pairs.bottom: May the peoples praise you, God; may all the peoples praise you (v.5)

center.label: X
center.text: May the nations be glad and sing for joy, for you rule the peoples with equity and guide the nations of the earth (v.4). The exact center of seven verses: God’s just rule over all nations is the ground of universal joy.

hebtext.note: "May God be gracious to us and bless us (yəbārek̅ēnū) and make his face shine on us — so that your ways may be known on earth, your salvation among all nations" (vv.1–2). The Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24–26) turned outward: Israel is blessed SO THAT the nations may know God. Blessing is not terminal but instrumental. The refrain: "May the peoples praise you, God; may all the peoples praise you" (vv.3, 5).

"The land yields its harvest (yəbūl); God, our God, blesses us" (v.6). The harvest as evidence of blessing. But the point is not the harvest itself: "May God bless us still, so that all the ends of the earth will fear him" (v.7). The final word is global: all the ends of the earth. The harvest is a sign; the mission is the point.

themes.note: Mission peaks at 10: the OT’s most concentrated missionary psalm. Covenant: blessed to be a blessing. Prophecy: all nations praising God.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'May God be gracious to us and bless us,' consider asking God whether you understand that His blessing is not an end in itself — it is given so that His ways may be known on earth, His salvation among all nations.
